data.title: Biggest YTD losses among large-cap software stocks with A profitability grades
data.description:
As AI continues to disrupt the software market, below is a list of the 10 software stocks with A profitability grades and large-market capitalizations, which have the worst price performance YTD. The list focuses on Application Software and Systems Software companies based in the United States.

The list is topped by AppLovin (APP), with a YTD performance of -45.55%. HubSpot (HUBS) and Figma (FIG) follow closely, with YTD losses of -42.95% and -41.08% respectively.

Other notable companies on the list include Intuit (INTU) and Guidewire Software (GWRE), which have experienced significant YTD declines. The list also features established industry leaders such as Salesforce (CRM) and ServiceNow (NOW), both of which have seen losses exceeding 30% year-to-date.

Here is the list:

  1. AppLovin (APP), YTD perf: -45.55%, Quant Rating: Hold 2.93

  2. HubSpot (HUBS), YTD perf: -42.95%, Quant Rating: Strong Sell 1.34

  3. Figma (FIG), YTD perf: -41.08%, Quant Rating: Not Covered

  4. Intuit (INTU), YTD perf: -39.92%, Quant Rating: Hold 2.83

  5. Guidewire Software (GWRE), YTD perf: -37.64%, Quant Rating: Strong Sell 1.30

  6. Tyler Technologies (TYL), YTD perf: -36.77%, Quant Rating: Sell 1.53

  7. Workday (WDAY), YTD perf: -32.94%, Quant Rating: Hold 3.22

  8. ServiceNow (NOW), YTD perf: -32.57%, Quant Rating: Hold 3.16

  9. Rubrik (RBRK), YTD perf: -30.45%, Quant Rating: Hold 3.19

  10. Salesforce (CRM), YTD perf: -30.00%, Quant Rating: Hold 3.38

Software ETFs: (IGPT), (XSW), (AOTS)

data.title: ICON rebounds as Kerrisdale Capital invests after selloff
data.description:
Shares of ICON Public Limited Company (ICLR) climbed ~17% on Friday after Kerrisdale Capital put 20% of its capital into the contract research organization, following a selloff that it said was “far too dramatic.”

Icon (ICLR) lost nearly 40% to hit a new 52-week low on Thursday, recording its worst intraday decline in more than two decades after the Ireland-based firm withdrew its 2025 outlook, citing an investigation into some of its accounting practices.

The company said that its 2023 and 2024 revenue figures may have been overstated by less than 2% due to potential “material weaknesses” in certain internal controls related to its financial reporting.

Icon’s (ICLR) market capitalization dropped to $6.1B after the selloff, which, according to Kerrisdale Capital, is “far too dramatic” for a CRO company of that size and scale.

“The -40% decline on an accounting restatement impacting <2% of revenue was far too dramatic for a leading CRO that gushes $1b of cash flow on now a $6b market cap,” the private investment manager said in an X post, adding, “We put 20% of our capital” into the stock on Thursday.

data.title: Charles Schwab records 18% Y/Y rise in January-end client assets
data.description:
Charles Schwab (SCHW) on Friday said its client assets totaled $12.15 trillion as of January-end, up 18% from January 2025 and 2% compared to December 2025.

Core net new assets at the end of January stood at $27.8B. The flows reflect typical January seasonality.

Transactional sweep cash decreased by $20.4B month-over-month to $433.3M, reflecting expected January seasonality.

A total of 476,000 new brokerage accounts were opened during the month, up 10% year-over-year.

January's daily average trades stood at 9.5M, driven by increased investor engagement in equities. Margin loan balances at the end of the month were $116.3B, an increase of 4% from year-end levels.

data.title: Wall Street moves higher after the latest CPI report
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages moved higher on Friday as traders digest the latest round of key retail inflation data.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was last higher by +0.4%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) moved up +0.5%, and the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) advanced by +0.3%. 

From a sector standpoint, nine of the S&P 500’s 11 segments were in positive trading territory, with utilities at the top. Going the other way, communication services has been the weakest performer. 

U.S. Treasury yields shifted lower across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell to 4.05%, and the 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) was down 4 basis points to 4.69%. The shorter-end 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) fell 5 basis points to 3.40%. 

On the economic front, the January Consumer Price Index came in at +0.2% M/M versus the +0.3% consensus and +0.3% prior readings. At the same time, core CPI (excludes food and energy) arrived at +0.3% M/M versus the +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior level. 

“Friday's delayed CPI for January was muted and in-line with expectations and it won't increase the likelihood of a rate cut within the next few months largely because of Wednesday's blowout employment numbers, which threw ice cold water on any hopes of a near-term rate cut.  The Fed is always in a tug of war between balancing inflation with employment, but they just can't cut rates right now with the economy having just created a six-figure jobs number,” Skyler Weinand, chief investment officer, of Regan Capital said.

As for stocks that were on the move, shares of Applied Materials (AMAT) climbed 8.8%, and shares of Norwegian Cruise Line (NCLH) came down by 6.7%.

data.title: Anthropic adds former Trump administration official Liddell to board ahead of IPO
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) has added former Trump administration official Chris Liddell to its board of directors ahead of a potential initial public offering, the company confirmed on Friday.

Liddell, who was the White House Deputy Chief of Staff in the first Trump administration, was also an executive at Microsoft (MSFT) and GM (GM). News of Liddell's appointment was first reported by The Wall Street Journal.

“Chris has spent his career at the intersection of technology, public service, and governance—and has a track record of helping organizations get those things right when the stakes are highest,” said Daniela Amodei, Co-founder and President of Anthropic, in a statement. “As AI’s impact on society grows, that kind of judgement and experience is exactly what we seek on our board.”

“My career has taught me the governance of transformative technologies matters as much as the technologies themselves,” Liddell added. “Anthropic’s approach—building AI that’s both capable and responsible—is critical for our future, and it’s why I’m proud to be joining the company’s Board.”

On Thursday, Anthropic announced it secured $30B in new funding at a $380B valuation. The Series G funding round includes several asset managers and some of the previously announced money from Microsoft and Nvidia (NVDA).

In addition, Anthropic said that the company's revenue run rate is now $14B, and this figure has grown more than 10 times annually over the past three years. Anthropic's revenue run rate surpassed $9B at the end of 2025, according to previous reporting. Included in that is a surge in Claude Code, the company's agentic AI coding tool. Claude Code's revenue run rate is now more than $2.5B and has more than doubled since the start of the year, Anthropic added. Weekly active users for Claude Code have also doubled since Jan. 1, Anthropic explained.

(This story has been updated to reflect Anthropic has confirmed the news.)

data.title: Eversource outlines $26.5B capital plan and targets 5–7% EPS growth through 2028 as regulatory clarity improves
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eversource Energy (ES) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Joseph Nolan, President, CEO & Chairman, stated that "2025 was another year of strong execution across the organization" and highlighted full year non-GAAP earnings per share of $4.76, along with dividends of $3.01 per share, a 5.2% increase. He emphasized operational performance, capital investments surpassing $4 billion, and significant progress on smart meter installations in Massachusetts, with over 100,000 meters deployed. Nolan also discussed successful collaboration with regulators and policymakers, particularly around affordability initiatives and constructive rate outcomes, and the completion of the onshore substation for the Revolution Wind project.
  • Nolan announced a new 5-year capital investment plan of $26.5 billion, increasing infrastructure investment by $2.3 billion, primarily targeting electric and natural gas distribution. "This new plan increases our necessary infrastructure investment over the next 5 years by $2.3 billion."
  • He confirmed the company’s strategy to continue regulatory engagement, the anticipated sale of Aquarion Water Company, and the pursuit of storm cost recovery in Connecticut, noting, "We are driving forward on several major fronts... executing relentlessly on completing our offshore wind commitments, enhancing storm cost securitization and managing the potential sale of Aquarion."
  • John Moreira, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer, reported GAAP earnings of $4.56 per share for 2025, including a net loss of $75 million related to the offshore wind sale, and non-GAAP earnings of $4.76 per share. He stated, "Our revised non-GAAP earnings guidance for 2025 was in the range of $4.72 to $4.80." Moreira also detailed segment performance, regulatory decisions, and financing needs, emphasizing the 5-year capital plan and the company’s intent to maintain a cushion over rating agency downgrade thresholds.

Outlook

  • The company projects 2026 earnings per share in the range of $4.80 to $4.95, with earnings growth expected to be more moderate due to timing of regulatory outcomes and headwinds related to Aquarion and storm cost recovery. Moreira explained, "For 2026, we expect earnings growth to be more moderate due primarily to the timing of key regulatory outcomes."
  • Eversource expects a meaningful inflection in earnings growth in 2027 and 2028, supported by improved regulatory outcomes, storm cost recovery, and distribution rate adjustments. The company projects a 5-year long-term EPS growth rate of 5% to 7%, based off 2025 non-GAAP recurring EPS of $4.76 per share, with an expectation to reach the upper half of this range by 2028.
  • The $26.5 billion capital plan reflects increased investments in electric distribution, natural gas distribution, transmission, and technology, excluding Aquarion Water, which would add $1.3 billion if included.

Financial Results

  • Eversource reported non-GAAP earnings of $4.76 per share for 2025, compared to $4.57 per share in 2024. Segment results included electric transmission earnings of $2.09 per share, electric distribution earnings of $1.80 per share, and natural gas distribution earnings of $0.97 per share.
  • The company improved its FFO-to-debt ratio at Moody’s by more than 400 basis points over the last 12 months, with S&P improvement of 300 basis points.
  • The capital investment plan for 2026–2030 totals $26.5 billion, including over $11 billion for electric distribution, nearly $7 billion for natural gas distribution, and over $7 billion for transmission.
  • Regulatory outcomes included PBR rate adjustments, cost recovery settlements, and the approved roll-in of storm and deferred costs in Massachusetts. The company is seeking a preliminary rate request of $88 million in additional revenues for Aquarion in Connecticut.

Q&A

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo: Asked about the impact of the Aquarion sale and storm cost recoveries on funding needs and growth trajectory. Moreira responded that equity needs of $0.8 billion to $1.1 billion are not affected by the Aquarion sale, while alternative financing would be adjusted if the sale is completed. "If Aquarion happens, then the alternative financing solutions will be pulled back."
  • Pourreza also questioned post-close liabilities related to Revolution Wind. Nolan clarified, "The trigger is COD, we deliver just as we did with South Fork. We feel very comfortable with the number that we're carrying now."
  • Carly Davenport, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about minority interest sales and Connecticut AMI investment timing. Moreira described several financing alternatives under consideration. Nolan noted a meeting next week to clarify AMI investment recovery and reiterated that investment would proceed only with a satisfactory recovery mechanism.
  • William Appicelli, UBS: Sought clarity on EPS growth base year and tax benefits from South Fork. Moreira confirmed the upper half of the growth range will be based off 2027 earnings, and that ITC credits remain unused for 2026.
  • Sophie Karp, KeyBanc: Requested timeline details for Revolution Wind’s COD. Nolan replied, "We're targeting the second half of 2026."
  • Paul Patterson, Glenrock Associates: Asked about incremental equity needs if Aquarion is not sold and about the Eversource Gas benefit. Moreira explained the impact on financing needs and that integration cost recoveries are reflected in the parent company.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were focused on funding flexibility, regulatory clarity, and the impact of pending transactions, with a generally neutral to slightly positive tone as questions sought detailed clarification on financing, project timelines, and regulatory processes.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing clarity, flexibility, and proactive engagement—"We feel very, very comfortable about our future," Nolan stated. Management was forthcoming with specifics and acknowledged uncertainties while expressing optimism about resolving key overhangs.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted from cautious optimism about regulatory changes and execution to more precise, scenario-based queries. Management’s tone remained confident, but demonstrated increased transparency and readiness to adapt to evolving circumstances.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a significant increase in capital investment planning, from a $24.2 billion plan to $26.5 billion over five years, and highlighted the achievement of higher non-GAAP earnings per share.
  • Management placed greater emphasis on derisking the business, strengthening the balance sheet, and resolving key open items, while the previous quarter focused on regulatory progress and operational achievements.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from broad regulatory and operational concerns to direct inquiries about funding strategies, project timelines, and the incremental impact of pending outcomes like the Aquarion sale and storm cost recovery.
  • Management’s confidence remained steady, but the current quarter reflected more detail on financing options, regulatory proceedings, and execution milestones.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited uncertainties regarding the sale of Aquarion Water and storm cost recoveries in Connecticut, emphasizing ongoing regulatory engagement and contingency planning.
  • The potential for higher financing needs if the Aquarion sale is not completed was acknowledged, with a focus on alternative funding solutions and maintaining financial discipline.
  • Analysts raised concerns about project timelines, regulatory approval processes, and the structure of potential minority interest sales. Management reiterated that investment decisions—particularly for Connecticut AMI—would be made only with approved recovery mechanisms.

Final Takeaway

Eversource management reiterated their focus on delivering operational excellence, advancing critical infrastructure investments, and maintaining financial flexibility. The company expects a transitional year in 2026 with moderate earnings growth, but projects a clear path to the upper half of its 5% to 7% long-term EPS growth range by 2028, underpinned by regulatory clarity, disciplined execution, and a strengthened balance sheet. Key milestones, including the resolution of Aquarion and storm cost recovery, are expected to provide further upside in the outer years of the forecast period.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Healthcare Realty outlines $1.61 FFO midpoint for 2026 while strengthening balance sheet and targeting NOI growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Healthcare Realty Trust Incorporated (HR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Peter Scott stated, “2025 was a transformational year at Healthcare Realty 2.0...We have the best-in-class portfolio. We have scale in the right markets, and we are aligned with leading health systems.” Scott highlighted the completion of the asset management platform revamp, noting, “cash leasing spreads have improved 60 basis points, tenant retention has improved 220 basis points, and we see a meaningful uptick in our lease IRRs and lease payback period.”
  • Scott reported the achievement of $10 million run rate G&A savings, with total G&A expense at $45 million, and completion of the asset disposition plan with $1.2 billion of assets sold at a 6.7% cap rate, exiting 14 noncore markets. “We are confident we have the premier outpatient medical portfolio, confirmed by the nearly 5% same-store NOI growth number that we generated in 2025.”
  • Scott further noted improvements in the balance sheet: “We have reduced net debt to EBITDA nearly a full turn to 5.4x...We also took the long overdue step to rightsize our dividend.”
  • The company completed its leadership overhaul and welcomed Dan Gabbay as CFO: “Dan and I have known each other for 20 years...He brings an exceptional blend of financial acumen, strategic insight and capital markets expertise.”
  • CFO Daniel Gabbay stated, “2025 ended strong. In Q4, we reported normalized FFO per share of $0.40 and same-store cash NOI growth of 5.5%. Additionally, FAD per share was $0.32, resulting in a quarterly dividend payout ratio of 75%.”

Outlook

  • The midpoint of 2026 normalized FFO guidance is $1.61 per share. Scott said, “embedded within the midpoint of our guidance is approximately 5% core earnings growth, which offsets the necessary dilution we proactively incurred from our back-end weighted 2025 dispositions and deleveraging.”
  • Same-store cash NOI growth for 2026 is projected at 3.5% to 4.5%. Gabbay confirmed, “We are forecasting normalized FFO per share of $1.58 to $1.64 representing $1.61 at the midpoint. These results are driven by lease-up and positive releasing spreads in our core portfolio.”
  • G&A is expected between $43 million and $47 million. Guidance does not include additional acquisitions, developments, or incremental share repurchases.

Financial Results

  • Q4 reported normalized FFO per share was $0.40. Same-store cash NOI growth reached 5.5%. Full-year normalized FFO per share was $1.61, with FAD per share at $1.26 and same-store cash NOI growth of 4.8%.
  • Q4 included nearly $700 million in dispositions; proceeds primarily used to pay off 2027 term loans. Total debt repaid was $900 million. Leverage decreased to 5.4x from 6.4x at the beginning of the year.
  • The company repurchased $50 million of stock in January 2026 and has $450 million remaining under current authorization.
  • Commercial paper program of $600 million was announced to further diversify capital sources.

Q&A

  • Juan Sanabria, BMO Capital Markets: Asked about 2026 same-store NOI guidance and drivers. Scott explained, “we're going to push the envelope across the 4 main drivers, and those drivers are escalators, retention, absorption and cash leasing spreads...our cash leasing spreads have ticked up in the second half of the year.”
  • Nicholas Yulico, Scotiabank: Inquired on redevelopment project timing and earnings impact. Scott indicated, “the real benefits don't start until 2027, but I would say our confidence level in our earnings framework is certainly increasing as we continue to lease up within that bucket.”
  • Nicholas Joseph, Citi: Questioned future disposition plans. Scott responded, “we've got $175 million of sales embedded within our guidance for this year...we expect to have all of that done in the very, very near term.”
  • Austin Wurschmidt, KeyBanc: Asked about retention assumptions and leasing pipeline. Scott stated, “I would say that we are expecting retention to be kind of in the 80% to 85% range. It will ebb and flow a little bit.” Hull added, “the 1.3 million square foot pipeline...is an active pipeline that is growing every day.”
  • Michael Gorman, BTIG: Asked about CapEx as a percentage of NOI. Scott answered, “it's probably 15% to 20%...But I think that's a good run rate number.”
  • William John Kilichowski, Wells Fargo: Sought clarity on NOI upside capture from redevelopment and margin expansion. Scott estimated, “I think we've probably identified about $15 million of the $50 million at this point in time through the leasing activity we've done since the strategic plan went out.”
  • Michael Mueller, JPMorgan: Inquired about redevelopment scope. Scott clarified, “the average project is probably in the $10 million area and it's probably in the $200 to $300 a foot overall.”
  • Omotayo Okusanya, Deutsche Bank: Asked about build-to-suit and development activity. Scott noted, “development that gets done is definitely heavily pre-leased...the challenges with new developments today...is pretty significant.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was inquisitive but measured, with several questions focusing on guidance assumptions, redevelopment timing, capital allocation, and disposition pacing. No overtly negative sentiment was detected; questions sought clarification rather than expressing concern.
  • Management maintained a confident and transparent tone in both prepared remarks and responses, emphasizing discipline and execution. Scott used phrases like “we are confident,” “we see a compelling opportunity,” and acknowledged operational challenges openly.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a constructive tone, with management sounding incrementally more confident about progress on strategic initiatives and future earnings growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The 2026 normalized FFO per share guidance midpoint is unchanged at $1.61 compared to the revised 2025 guidance from last quarter, but management now emphasizes a 5% core earnings growth offset by dilution from asset sales and deleveraging.
  • Strategic focus has shifted from executing asset dispositions and restructuring to capital allocation, redevelopment, and disciplined growth. The asset management platform revamp and leadership changes were completed this quarter.
  • Analysts continued to focus on guidance, capital allocation, and pipeline visibility, with more pointed questions on the timing and magnitude of redevelopment NOI and retention assumptions.
  • Management’s confidence appeared higher, noting they are tracking ahead of schedule on strategic initiatives and that the company is “front-footed” heading into 2026.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the current cost of capital and discount to intrinsic asset value as limiting external growth, stating, “our capital allocation approach will remain incredibly disciplined.”
  • Increased cost of refinancing is expected, with Gabbay noting, “we assume the $600 million bond due this August, will be refinanced with new bonds in the low 5% coupon area...as compared to the existing coupon of 3.5%.”
  • Challenges with new development due to higher construction costs and rental rate requirements were highlighted.
  • Analyst questions indicated interest in the sustainability of same-store NOI growth, execution risk on redevelopments, and the pace and use of capital deployment.

Final Takeaway

Healthcare Realty closed a transformative year by completing leadership changes, executing its asset disposition plan, and revamping its asset management platform. The company reported strong leasing, improved retention, and a strengthened balance sheet, with leverage now at 5.4x. For 2026, guidance calls for normalized FFO per share at a $1.61 midpoint and same-store NOI growth of 3.5% to 4.5%. Redevelopment projects and disciplined capital allocation remain central to future growth, while management acknowledged ongoing constraints from cost of capital and market valuation. The company enters 2026 emphasizing operational execution, prudent investment, and a focus on long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Advance Auto Parts targets 130–200 basis points operating margin expansion in 2026 as strategic transformation accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Advance Auto Parts (AAP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Shane O'Kelly, President and CEO, stated the company is in a "significant transformation focused on the fundamentals of selling auto parts through initiatives guided by the voice of our customer." He highlighted a return to positive comparable sales growth after three years of declines and noted that adjusted operating income margin expanded by over 200 basis points from near breakeven levels. O'Kelly announced, "We expect these efforts to deliver stronger financial performance in 2026, including an acceleration in comparable sales growth to the 1% to 2% range and expansion in adjusted operating income margin to the 3.8% to 4.5% range and a return to positive free cash flow."

  • O'Kelly detailed key strategic initiatives in 2025, including exiting over 500 corporate and 200 independent underperforming locations, expanding assortment by 100,000 new SKUs, improving store availability, and reducing product costs. He said, "We now operate 16 distribution centers in the U.S. compared to nearly 40 DCs at the end of 2023," and "We opened 14 new market hubs and now operate 33 market hub locations."

  • The leadership team was strengthened with internal promotions and external hires, notably Anthony Sarlanis (SVP of Pro business), Kunal Das (CTO), Ron Gilbert (SVP of Supply Chain), and Tony Hurst (SVP of U.S. Stores).

  • O'Kelly reaffirmed the company's goal of achieving a 7% adjusted operating income margin with a mid-40% gross margin, stating, "We concluded 2025 with an adjusted operating income margin of 2.5%. And for 2026, we are targeting an additional 130 to 200 basis points of expansion to the 3.8% to 4.5% range."

  • O'Kelly highlighted the launch of a new owned oil and fluids brand, ARGOS, and the rollout of the updated Advance Rewards loyalty program, as well as continued investments in store upgrades and market hub expansion.

  • CFO Ryan Grimsland reported, "For the fourth quarter, net sales from continuing operations were approximately $2 billion, which declined 1% compared to last year... Comparable sales grew 1.1% in the fourth quarter." He added, "Adjusted operating income from continuing operations was $73 million, or 3.7% of net sales, resulting in nearly 870 basis points of year-over-year operating margin expansion."

Outlook

  • Grimsland guided for 2026 net sales to decline slightly year-over-year due to 2025 nonrecurring items, but underlying net sales are expected to grow 1% to 2%. Comparable sales growth is projected at 1% to 2% with Pro outpacing DIY.

  • Adjusted operating income margin for 2026 is forecasted between 3.8% and 4.5%, with gross margin expansion to approximately 45%. Adjusted diluted EPS is expected in the range of $2.40 to $3.10. Free cash flow is projected to be approximately $100 million.

  • Grimsland noted, "We expect positive comp growth in each quarter with a stronger first half owing to easier comparisons."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter net sales from continuing operations were $2 billion. Comparable sales rose 1.1%. Adjusted gross profit was $873 million, or 44.2% of net sales, with nearly 530 basis points of gross margin expansion year-over-year.

  • Adjusted SG&A from continuing operations was $800 million, or 40.5% of net sales. Adjusted diluted earnings per share from continuing operations was $0.86 compared to a loss of ($1.18) last year. For the full year, adjusted diluted EPS was $2.26 versus a loss of ($0.29) in 2024.

  • Free cash flow for the year was negative $298 million, impacted by $140 million in store optimization expenses and $160 million in other outflows.

  • Net debt leverage improved to 2.4x at year end.

Q&A

  • Christopher Horvers, JPMorgan: Asked about SKU inflation compared to peers and the rationale behind supply chain financing decisions. Grimsland responded that "our SKU inflation, I think, is consistent with peers," and explained payables reduction was due to "leveling the payables based on the new purchases that we have."

  • Seth Sigman, Barclays: Inquired about the impact of store closings and real estate optimization. Grimsland clarified that liquidations had a $51 million impact and stated, "No further closures we expect."

  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Questioned execution risk for margin gains. Grimsland said, "On the merch side, we've got really good line of sight. We've made a lot of great progress this year."

  • Bret Jordan, Jefferies: Asked about private label strategy and market hub rollout. Grimsland indicated private label should remain around 50% of sales and that most new market hubs will be greenfield locations.

  • Mark Jordan, Goldman Sachs: Sought details on comp guidance and transaction trends. Grimsland noted, "DIY transactions, we'd expect to be pressured and continued... the pressure is on the DIY side there."

  • Zachary Fadem, Wells Fargo: Asked about vendor finance and LIFO expectations. Grimsland responded, "LIFO expense for '26, which is in our guidance, about 50 basis points of headwind that we'll see."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a slightly positive tone, congratulating management on progress but pressing for detail on margin targets, execution risks, and the cadence of strategic initiatives. Questions frequently focused on sustainability and execution of margin expansion.

  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident and focused on operational progress, while in Q&A, responses remained measured and detailed, using phrases like "we are being prudent" and "we feel good about the leaders who sit in the seats."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone has shifted from emphasizing the "nonlinear" nature of turnaround to providing more concrete milestones and forward-looking projections. Analysts continue to press for evidence of sustainable improvement but acknowledge operational progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more explicit, with operating income margin targets raised to 3.8%–4.5% and comparable sales growth projected at 1%–2%, whereas Q3 guidance reaffirmed 2.4%–2.6% margins and 0.7%–1.3% comp sales growth.

  • Strategic focus has shifted to execution of merchandising, supply chain consolidation, and store operations, with an increased emphasis on leadership changes and proprietary technology adoption.

  • Analysts' focus remained on the sustainability of margin gains, the impact of store closures, real estate optimization, and private label expansion.

  • Management confidence has increased with the articulation of specific 2026 and 2027 targets and acknowledgment of execution risks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued volatility in consumer spending, external tariff challenges, and headwinds from LIFO expense as ongoing risks.

  • Grimsland emphasized that DIY transactions remain under pressure and that macroeconomic factors could impact sales trends, but the company is investing in loyalty and private label to mitigate this.

  • Analyst concerns centered on the sustainability of margin expansion, execution risk in strategic sourcing, and the impact of store closures on core sales.

Final Takeaway

Advance Auto Parts laid out concrete milestones for 2026, projecting notable operating margin expansion and a return to positive free cash flow, while emphasizing the execution of merchandising, supply chain, and store initiatives. Management remains confident in the company’s transformation and is focusing on operational discipline, leadership, and technology to drive sustainable growth and margin improvement, despite ongoing macroeconomic and industry-specific risks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alibaba and Baidu added -- then removed -- from Pentagon's Section 1260H list
data.description:
Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU) suffered a brief sell-off early Friday after the Department of Defense issued—and then quickly retracted—a list of companies that are aiding the Chinese military.

After pulling the list of targeted companies, the Pentagon issued a statement to Bloomberg that it had “nothing to announce at this time,” raising questions if the retraction was intentional before President Trump’s visit to China in April or whether it was posted in error.

Along with the addition of Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU), the Pentagon removed ChangXin Memory Technologies – China’s largest manufacturer of memory chips --  and CCP-backed Yangtze Memory Technologies  from Section 1260H restrictions, enabling the adoption of Chinese DRAM and NAND in U.S. consumer products.  

Companies that landed on the Pentagon’s Section 1260H list were labeled as “Communist Chinese Military Companies,” considered threats to U.S. security, and prevented from operating in the U.S., either directly or indirectly, and restricted from receiving research funding. The list now contains more than 130 entities accused of associations with China’s military.

Both Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU) reject any claim of military association and maintain that products on their respective websites are “designed for civilian use only.”

“We are a publicly listed company...and will not hesitate to use all options available to us to have the company removed from the list,” Baidu said to Bloomberg.  

Moreover, China’s Foreign Ministry said it will “take necessary measures to resolutely safeguard the legitimate rights and interests of Chinese enterprises.”

data.title: ETF inflows roar out of the gate in 2026 as they are on pace to top $2T by year end
data.description:
Exchange traded funds are off to a historic start in 2026, with investors pouring roughly $250B into ETFs in just the first 28 trading days of the year.

At that pace—about $9B per day—translates to an annualized run rate of approximately $2.2T. 

To put the surge in perspective, before 2020, $250B represented the average inflow for an entire calendar year. This year’s acceleration more than doubles any previous start on record, underscoring strong investor demand for low-cost, liquid exposure across equities and fixed income. 

Large-cap U.S. equity exposure is leading the charge, while international and emerging markets funds are also seeing substantial interest. At the same time, bond ETFs continue to attract steady allocations, reflecting balanced positioning amid evolving rate expectations.
